author: Petrik
message:
Change the order of Views and Controllers in the guides

The guides currently mention the ActionView guides before the
ActionController guides. This makes sense if the order is MVC.

However, some of the ActionView guides require knowledge of controllers
and routes, whereas most of the ActionController guides can be read
without knowledge of ActionView. Should we change the order and mention
controllers and routing first?

Xavier proposed using the request lifecycle order: route -> controller
-> view -> response.

This moves "Controllers" before "Views", renames "Controllers" to
"Routing and Controllers", and moves the routing guide above the
controllers guides.

author: Petrik
message:
Comment ellipsis in guides examples

Copying an example should not output the plain ellipsis.
This is problematic in Ruby where `...` is valid Ruby, while in other
languages it will generate syntax errors.

For output of Rails commands we can still use regular ellipsis as
copying that output won't work anyway.

This also updates the guides guidelines with an example.

author: Lemayian JN
message:
Fix settings navigation test in authentication guide

The guide checks for the absence of "Store Settings" using
`assert_not_dom "a", "Store Settings"`.

However, "Store Settings" is rendered as an `h4` heading rather
than a link, so the selector should be `h4` to match the markup
shown in the guide.

Downcase Cache-Control key in asset pipeline guide example

author: meganemura
message:
Downcase Cache-Control key in asset pipeline guide example

#52653 downcased the cache-control header key in the generated
development.rb.tt and test.rb.tt templates so newly generated apps
follow the Rack 3 SPEC, which requires response headers to be
lowercase. The asset pipeline guide's CDN section still showed the
config.public_file_server.headers example with the old
"Cache-Control" casing, so update it to match the generated code.

author: fatkodima
message:
Merge pull request #58096 from 55728/fix-sqlite3-foreign-key-name

Read foreign key names from the schema on SQLite3

author: Kenta Ishizaki
message:
Read foreign key names from the schema on SQLite3

On SQLite3, `foreign_keys` did not read the constraint name from the
table definition, so every `ForeignKeyDefinition` came back with
`name == nil`. Several user-visible bugs followed:

- `remove_foreign_key(table, name: "fk")` ignored the name and removed
  an arbitrary (the first) foreign key instead of the named one, and
  `remove_foreign_key(..., name:, if_exists: true)` with a non-matching
  name removed a foreign key rather than being a no-op.
- Any schema change that rebuilds the table (a second `add_foreign_key`,
  `add_column`, ...) regenerated a custom foreign key name into
  `fk_rails_...`, and custom names were dropped from the schema dump.

`foreign_keys` now captures the constraint name, and SQLite3
`remove_foreign_key` honors `:name` when matching, aligning with the
abstract adapter and MySQL/PostgreSQL. Custom names now survive table
rebuilds and round-trip through the schema dump; auto-generated
`fk_rails_*` names remain ignored by the dumper as before.

author: fatkodima
message:
Merge pull request #58077 from 55728/fix-query-command-line-comment-strip

Strip only the commented line when detecting a LIMIT in `rails query`

author: Kenta Ishizaki
message:
Strip only the commented line when detecting a LIMIT in rails query

`rails query --sql` decides whether to append its own `LIMIT` by first
removing comments from the SQL. The line-comment pattern was `--.*$` with
the `/m` flag (added for block comments), so `.` matched newlines and a
single `--` comment consumed the rest of the statement, hiding a real
`LIMIT` that followed it. The command then appended a second `LIMIT`,
producing invalid SQL. The line-comment pattern now stops at the newline.

author: fatkodima
message:
Merge pull request #58092 from 55728/fix-relative-time-in-words-non-time-inputs

Accept `Date` and numeric inputs in `relative_time_in_words`

author: Kenta Ishizaki
message:
Accept Date and numeric inputs in relative_time_in_words

Passing a Date or a Numeric raised ArgumentError instead of returning a
distance string, unlike distance_of_time_in_words which accepts them.

author: fatkodima
message:
Merge pull request #58091 from 55728/fix-sqlite3-add-check-constraint-if-not-exists

Honor `if_not_exists:` in SQLite3 `add_check_constraint` and `add_foreign_key`

author: Kenta Ishizaki
message:
Honor if_not_exists in SQLite3 add_check_constraint and add_foreign_key

add_check_constraint and add_foreign_key accept if_not_exists: true to make
adding a constraint or foreign key that already exists a no-op instead of an
error. The abstract adapter (used by MySQL and Trilogy) and the SQLite3
remove_check_constraint and remove_foreign_key overrides all honor their
existence option, but the SQLite3 add_check_constraint and add_foreign_key
overrides never declared if_not_exists:, so the option fell into **options
unused and a duplicate was added.

They now return early when the constraint or foreign key already exists,
matching the abstract adapter and the remove overrides.

author: fatkodima
message:
Merge pull request #58086 from 55728/fix-hwia-transform-keys-symbol-mapping

Convert Symbol keys in a `HashWithIndifferentAccess#transform_keys` mapping

author: Kenta Ishizaki
message:
Convert Symbol keys in a HashWithIndifferentAccess#transform_keys mapping

transform_keys / transform_keys! accept a hash that maps old keys to new ones.
Under indifferent access the mapping keys were matched against the string-stored
keys without conversion, so a Symbol-keyed mapping never matched and the rename
was silently a no-op — even though every other key-argument entry point (slice,
dig, fetch, []) converts the key, and a plain Hash renames the same way.

  { a: 1, b: 2 }.with_indifferent_access.transform_keys(a: :x)
  # before => { "a" => 1, "b" => 2 }   (unchanged)
  # after  => { "x" => 1, "b" => 2 }

String-keyed mappings, block forms, and the combined hash + block form are
unchanged, as is the TypeError raised for a non-hash argument.

author: kyuuri1791
message:
Fix MySQL POINT and MULTIPOINT columns being misreported as integers

Both type names contain the substring "int", so they matched the
generic %r(int)i rule in AbstractAdapter's type map and came back
with type: :integer, instead of being treated as an unknown type
like the other spatial types (GEOMETRY, POLYGON, LINESTRING, ...).

Register the two names explicitly in the abstract MySQL adapter,
where they take precedence over the generic rule and cover both
mysql2 and trilogy. The generic rule itself is left untouched since
other adapters rely on unanchored substring matching.

author: Kenta Ishizaki
message:
Round-trip the null: false attribute modifier in GeneratedAttribute#to_s

The `!` modifier on a generator attribute is stored as `null: false`, but
`GeneratedAttribute#to_s` had no textual form for it. `name:string!`
serialized to `name:string{null}` (which no longer parses as that type);
`name:integer{4}!` dropped the modifier entirely; and
`name:references{polymorphic}!` produced `{polymorphic,null}`, which
parses back as `null: true` — inverting the constraint. `to_s` now emits
the trailing `!`, so parsing an attribute and serializing it round-trips.

author: Kenta Ishizaki
message:
Interpolate a fixture label verbatim, without backreference expansion

`$LABEL` in a fixture value was replaced with the label via
`gsub("$LABEL", @label.to_s)`, where the label is the string replacement
argument. `gsub` interprets `\0`-`\9`, `\&`, and `\k<...>` in a string
replacement as backreferences, so a label containing such a sequence was
expanded instead of inserted literally (e.g. a `\&` label produced the
matched `$LABEL` text). The block form inserts the label verbatim.
